_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Ware County, GA. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses. Ware County, formed 1824 from Appling County, is the oldest county in the cluster. Waycross as county seat from the 1870s (previously at Waresboro). Records from 1824 with no major fire — one of the longer continuous record runs in the Wiregrass.

previous_courthouses.fate: County seat moved to Waycross in 1874 when the railroad made Waycross the dominant town. Records transferred.

record_losses.cause: County seat relocation from Waresboro to Waycross
records_destroyed: Possible minor losses during physical transfer
record_losses.description: Ware County moved its seat from Waresboro to Waycross in 1874, following the railroad. Records were transferred successfully. The move itself resulted in minimal loss, though some handling damage to early volumes is evident. Records from 1824 are the oldest in the cluster and are in fair condition given their age.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Ware County's records extend to 1824 — the longest continuous run in the Wiregrass cluster. If {ancestor_name} was in Ware County from the county's formation, the paper trail is remarkably complete for such a remote frontier county.
record_losses.source: GA Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.

deeds.completeness: Good from formation. Early volumes show age but are legible.
deeds.repository: Ware County Clerk of Superior Court; GA Archives microfilm

wills_estates.completeness: Fair to Good from the 1820s. Early estates are simple — small farms, livestock, turpentine equipment.
wills_estates.gaps: Low volume in early decades due to sparse population.
wills_estates.repository: Ware County Probate Court; GA Archives

marriages.completeness: Good from 1854. Pre-1854 marriages not systematically recorded.
marriages.gaps: 1824-1854 marriages largely unrecorded at county level.

court_minutes.repository: Ware County Clerk of Superior Court; GA Archives

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until mid-1920s. Swamp communities especially underregistered.

substitute_sources.description: The families who lived on the Okefenokee's island hammocks — Billy's Island, Cowhouse Island, Chesser Island — left records in the form of family papers, oral histories collected by folklorists, and US Fish and Wildlife Service documentation from the refuge establishment in 1937. Francis Harper's fieldwork in the 1910s-1920s documented swamp families in detail.
substitute_sources.repository: Okefenokee National Wildlife Refuge archives; University of Georgia Hargrett Library (Harper papers)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The only source for families who lived in the swamp rather than in the settled areas near Waycross. The swamp families (Lee, Chesser, Crews, Hickox) are documented in Harper's work and in the refuge relocation records.
substitute_sources.source: Harper, Francis, and Delma E. Presley. Okefinokee Album. Athens: University of Georgia Press, 1981.

substitute_sources.description: The Hebard Lumber Company operated the largest cypress logging operation in the Okefenokee from 1909-1927. Company records include employee rolls, payroll records, and timber cutting records.
substitute_sources.repository: Waycross-Ware County Library; some records at GA Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Documents workers in the swamp logging operation, many of whom were Black men including convict-lease laborers. The company records may be the only source for laborers who do not appear in other county records.
substitute_sources.source: Trowell, C. T. Exploring the Okefenokee (2007).

substitute_sources.description: Covers Ware County families extensively — Huxford was from Homerville in adjacent Clinch County and knew the Wiregrass families personally.
substitute_sources.repository: Major Georgia libraries; Huxford Genealogical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: One of the most detailed compilations. Huxford's personal knowledge of Wiregrass families makes his Ware County entries particularly reliable.

narrative_hooks: Ware County's records go back to 1824 — the oldest in the Wiregrass cluster. If {ancestor_name} was there from the beginning, the paper trail extends further than almost any other county in south Georgia.

If {ancestor_name} lived in the Okefenokee — on Billy's Island or Chesser Island or one of the other hammocks — the records are not at the courthouse but in the swamp community papers, Francis Harper's fieldwork, and the refuge relocation files.

The Hebard Lumber Company records may be the only documentation of {ancestor_name}'s work in the Okefenokee cypress — payroll records and employee rolls from a logging operation that extracted centuries-old trees from a swamp that no other records reach.

Ware County moved its seat from Waresboro to Waycross in 1874, following the railroad — the records made the journey by wagon, and the old county seat was abandoned to become a crossroads village that most maps forgot.
source_references: GA Department of Archives and History. Guide to County Records.
Harper, Francis, and Delma E. Presley. Okefinokee Album. Athens: University of Georgia Press, 1981.
Trowell, C. T. Exploring the Okefenokee: Roland Harper's Naturalist Explorations. Macon: Mercer University Press, 2007.
Huxford, Folks. Pioneers of Wiregrass Georgia. 10 vols.
